title: Show the joints
summary: A German filmmaker spends four days with a Vietnamese stilt-house carpenter and learns that the film she came to make is not the right one.
body:
Last March I spent four days in a small village in northern Vietnam, in a Tày community, with a man named Ông Hùng. He is sixty-eight. He is a carpenter who builds stilt houses — homes raised on wooden posts above the ground, with one floor for animals below and one for the family above. He has built ninety-one of them over four decades. Forty-three are still standing. The rest were torn down for highways, hydroelectric projects, or because the families moved into concrete houses in town.

I am a documentary filmmaker from Berlin. I went to film Ông Hùng because I had seen one of his houses in a photograph and assumed — wrongly, as it turned out — that he was a romantic figure resisting modernity. He is not. He is a working carpenter who, when his clients ask for concrete, builds concrete. He has built three concrete houses in the past five years.

I asked him why he still builds stilt houses when most clients now want concrete. He did not say anything about tradition. He said the wood houses are better for the rice fields. Above the floods. Cool in summer. The buffalo lives downstairs and the floor is warmer in winter because of that. Concrete is colder and the family pays more in electricity. The carpenter is making an engineering argument, not a cultural one.

I had filmed seven hours by the third day. None of it felt right. I had been trying to film a story I had brought with me. On the morning of the fourth day, I asked Ông Hùng what he wanted to be on the film, if anything. He thought for a long time. He said: 'Show the joints. The joints are the work. Do not show me. Show how the wood meets the wood.'

I refilmed for three hours. The new footage is of joints. Sixty-eight joints. No carpenter visible. No voice-over. The film, when it is finished, will be eight minutes long.

I do not know if this is the right film. I know it is more honest than the one I came to make.

annotations.meaningVi: nhà sàn (kê trên cọc gỗ)
thợ mộc
mối nối / khớp gỗ
tính hiện đại / nền hiện đại
thuỷ điện
lời dẫn (giọng nói chèn vào phim)
bị phá dỡ / bị đập xuống (phrasal verb)
hoá ra / thực ra (khi sự thật hiện ra khác với dự đoán)
chống lại / phản kháng (không chấp nhận điều gì)
đạo diễn phim tài liệu
đoạn phim được quay / cảnh quay thô
lập luận dựa trên kỹ thuật / lý lẽ công trình học
trong hơn bốn thập kỷ (= hơn 40 năm)
ruộng lúa / đồng lúa
nếu có gì (dùng để hỏi hoặc nhấn nhẹ)
quay lại / quay thêm lần nữa
thợ mộc đang hành nghề / thợ mộc thực thụ (không phải nghệ nhân lãng mạn)
giả định / đoán (thường không có đủ bằng chứng)
trung thực hơn (so sánh hơn của 'honest')
chiếu các mối nối (câu của Ông Hùng: điều quan trọng nhất là kỹ thuật kết nối, không phải con người)

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for events before another past point
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng quá khứ hoàn thành ('had + V3') khi cần chỉ rõ điều gì đã xảy ra trước một thời điểm cũng ở quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I had filmed seven hours by the third day.' (bảy giờ phim trước ngày thứ ba) 'I had been trying to film a story I had brought with me.' (đã cố / đã mang theo). 'I had seen one of his houses in a photograph and assumed…' (đã xem trước khi đi). Cấu trúc tạo lớp thời gian — quá khứ-trong-quá-khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses past perfect ('had + past participle') to mark an event that happened before another past point. The passage: 'I had filmed seven hours by the third day.' 'I had been trying to film a story I had brought with me.' 'I had seen one of his houses in a photograph and assumed…' This creates a layered past — a past-before-the-past.

translationVi:
Tháng Ba năm ngoái tôi đã dành bốn ngày ở một làng nhỏ miền bắc Việt Nam, trong một cộng đồng Tày, với một người đàn ông tên Ông Hùng. Ông sáu mươi tám tuổi. Ông là thợ mộc làm nhà sàn — những căn nhà dựng trên cọc gỗ cao khỏi mặt đất, tầng dưới cho gia súc, tầng trên cho gia đình. Bốn thập kỷ qua ông đã làm chín mươi mốt căn. Bốn mươi ba căn vẫn còn đứng. Còn lại bị phá vì làm cao tốc, vì các dự án thuỷ điện, hoặc vì các gia đình chuyển vào nhà bê tông trong thị trấn.

Tôi là một nhà làm phim tài liệu từ Berlin. Tôi đi quay Ông Hùng vì tôi đã thấy một căn nhà của ông trong một bức ảnh và đoán — sai, như sau này hiện ra — rằng ông là một nhân vật lãng mạn chống lại sự hiện đại. Ông không phải. Ông là một thợ mộc đang làm việc, khi khách yêu cầu bê tông, ông làm bê tông. Năm năm qua ông đã làm ba căn nhà bê tông.

Tôi hỏi ông vì sao vẫn làm nhà sàn khi hầu hết khách giờ muốn bê tông. Ông không nói gì về truyền thống. Ông nói nhà gỗ tốt hơn cho ruộng lúa. Trên mực lũ. Mát mùa hè. Con trâu ở dưới và sàn nhà ấm hơn vào mùa đông vì nó. Bê tông lạnh hơn và gia đình tốn nhiều điện hơn. Người thợ mộc đang đưa ra một lập luận kỹ thuật, không phải văn hoá.

Đến ngày thứ ba tôi đã quay được bảy giờ phim. Không có giờ nào cảm thấy đúng. Tôi đã cố quay một câu chuyện mà tôi mang theo từ trước. Sáng ngày thứ tư, tôi hỏi Ông Hùng ông muốn cái gì có trong phim, nếu có. Ông suy nghĩ rất lâu. Ông nói: 'Quay các mối nối. Mối nối là phần việc. Đừng quay tôi. Quay cách gỗ gặp gỗ.'

Tôi quay lại ba giờ nữa. Hình mới là các mối nối. Sáu mươi tám mối. Không có người thợ mộc xuất hiện. Không có lời dẫn. Phim, khi xong, sẽ dài tám phút.

Tôi không biết đây có phải phim đúng không. Tôi biết nó trung thực hơn phim tôi đã đến để làm.

options: Stilt houses — wooden homes raised on posts with animals below and family above

questions.prompt: How many stilt houses has he built, and how many still stand?
Why were many of his houses torn down?
options: For highways, hydroelectric projects, or because families moved into concrete houses

questions.prompt: What did the filmmaker WRONGLY assume?
options: That he was a romantic figure resisting modernity.

questions.prompt: Has he built any concrete houses?
options: Yes — three in the past five years; he builds what clients ask for.

questions.prompt: Why, according to him, are stilt houses better?
options: Better for rice fields (above floods); cool in summer; the buffalo downstairs keeps the floor warmer in winter; concrete is colder and costs more in electricity.

questions.prompt: What kind of argument is the carpenter making?
options: An engineering argument, not a cultural one.

questions.prompt: Why did the first seven hours of footage not feel right?
options: The filmmaker had been trying to film a story she had brought with her.

questions.prompt: What did Ông Hùng ask her to film?
options: The joints — how the wood meets the wood. Not him.
Three hours refilmed: sixty-eight joints; no carpenter visible; no voice-over; about eight minutes when finished.
A long documentary about Vietnamese architecture

questions.prompt: What does the writer say at the end?
options: She does not know if it is the right film, but it is more honest than the one she came to make.
She has thrown the footage away.
She has filmed Ông Hùng's face after all.
She has returned to Berlin without filming.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: An outsider can arrive with a story that the subject does not own, and the more honest film begins when the subject is allowed to name what should actually be shown.
questions.explanation: The reframing of the film when Ông Hùng speaks.

cloze.template:
Last March I spent four days in a small village in northern Vietnam, in a Tày community, with a man named Ông Hùng. He is sixty-eight. He is a {{b1}} who builds stilt houses — homes raised on wooden posts above the ground, with one floor for animals below and one for the family above. He has built {{b2}} of them over four decades. Forty-three are still standing. The rest were torn down for highways, hydroelectric projects, or because the families moved into {{b3}} houses in town.

I am a documentary filmmaker from Berlin. I went to film Ông Hùng because I had seen one of his houses in a photograph and assumed — wrongly, as it turned out — that he was a romantic figure resisting {{b4}}. He is not. He is a working carpenter who, when his clients ask for concrete, builds concrete. He has built three concrete houses in the past five years.

I asked him why he still builds stilt houses when most clients now want concrete. He did not say anything about {{b5}}. He said the wood houses are better for the rice fields. Above the floods. Cool in summer. The buffalo lives downstairs and the floor is warmer in winter because of that. Concrete is colder and the family pays more in electricity. The carpenter is making an {{b6}} argument, not a cultural one.

I had filmed seven hours by the third day. None of it felt right. I had been trying to film a story I had brought with me. On the morning of the fourth day, I asked Ông Hùng what he wanted to be on the film, if anything. He thought for a long time. He said: 'Show the {{b7}}. The joints are the work. Do not show me. Show how the wood meets the wood.'

I refilmed for three hours. The new footage is of joints. Sixty-{{b8}} joints. No carpenter visible. No voice-over. The film, when it is finished, will be {{b9}} minutes long.

I do not know if this is the right film. I know it is more {{b10}} than the one I came to make.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The filmmaker realised she had brought her story with her, instead of letting Ông Hùng name what should be shown. Is there a place in your work or relationships where you have been telling someone else's story without asking them what they would actually want shown?
